disease: Posterior Semicircular Canal Dehiscence  due to a high jugular bulb
text: An 18-year-old female presented with progressive pulse-synchronous tinnitus in her right ear, which intensified during physical exertion and when lying down. She also reported aural fullness, autophony, and heightened sensitivity to her own body sounds on the same side. There was no history of head trauma or chronic conditions. Otoscopy results were normal. Pure-tone audiometry indicated a pseudo-conductive hearing loss in the right ear at low frequencies with negative bone conduction thresholds at 250 Hz, while the left ear had normal hearing. Impedance audiometry showed normal tympanometry and acoustic reflexes bilaterally. Despite the absence of vestibular symptoms, a full vestibular assessment was conducted. The Video-Frenzel examination, including head-shaking test and Valsalva maneuvers, was normal. However, the video head impulse test (vHIT) revealed a slight vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) gain impairment (0.67) for the right posterior semicircular canal. Cervical and ocular vestibular evoked myogenic potentials (VEMPs) to air-conducted sounds showed increased amplitudes and reduced thresholds on the right side.

A 58-year-old Japanese man experienced blurred vision one week after receiving the second dose of the COVID-19 mRNA vaccine (BNT162b2, Pfizer, Tokyo, Japan) in 2021. Three months later, he began to experience headache, nausea, and vertigo. An MRI scan of his head showed no abnormalities. Due to worsening blurred vision and the onset of tinnitus, he consulted an ophthalmologist and was found to have iritis and uveitis. He reported leukoderma on his head and was referred to our hospital nine months after his second vaccination. Upon examination, he had depigmentation on his bilateral forehead and forearm in multiple coalescing patches, as well as annular erythema on his bilateral upper arms. His medical history included pompholyx and atopic dermatitis. Laboratory tests revealed elevated serum levels of immunoglobulin E (IgE) at 1,561 IU/mL and thymus and activation-regulated chemokine (TARC) at 1,050 pg/mL. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone, free T3, and free T4 levels were within normal limits. Autoantibodies, including antinuclear antibody, anti-SS-A, and anti-SS-B antibody, were negative.

disease: Central Diabetes Insipidus secondary to Hantavirus Infection
text: A 49-year-old male presented to the hospital with severe headaches that began at the front and later spread to the back of the head, accompanied by sensitivity to light. For the past three days, he experienced severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and general malaise. Upon examination, he had normal body temperature, stable vital signs, no altered consciousness, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15. His history ruled out trauma or drug intoxication. Initial clinical and laboratory evaluations led to a computed tomography scan, which showed a mass in the sellar region. Subsequent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a hemorrhagic sellar mass. Laboratory tests on the fifth day after admission indicated insufficiency in the corticotropic, thyrotropic, and gonadotropic axes. Despite MRI showing a shift in the optic chiasm, ophthalmological examination revealed only minor, non-specific visual field defects without papilledema or orthoptic abnormalities. Surgical intervention was planned but postponed due to severe acute kidney injury, with a creatinine level of 11.6 mg/dL and an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of less than 20 mL/min on the fifth day after admission. The patient, a driving instructor, reported digging out a bamboo hedge before hospitalization. Further testing on the third day after admission confirmed hantavirus infection through detection of IgM and IgG antibodies against Puumala hantavirus using immunofluorescence and line immunoassays, as well as positive polymerase chain reaction results. Specific amplification and sequencing of the partial L-Segment identified a puumalavirus circulating in the Middle Rhine region in Germany. Serological tests ruled out infections by hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus, and HIV. The patient was transferred to the nephrology department and became oliguric. Due to increasing nausea and rising blood urea levels, haemodialysis was considered but kidney function began to recover, avoiding the need for renal replacement therapy. Symptomatic treatment and hormonal replacement led to increased urinary excretion by day 3 and decreased creatinine levels from day 6 onwards. No specific antiviral treatment was administered. Despite overall improvement, diuretic medication was withheld from day 5, achieving a negative fluid balance with continuous weight loss daily, but urine excretion remained high (6.25 mL/kg/h). Initially attributed to the polyuric phase of acute kidney injury recovery, persistent plasma hyperosmolality and urine hypoosmolality led to further evaluation.

A 56-year-old woman reported experiencing dull aching pain, swelling, and restricted movement in her left knee for the past 10 years. The pain, which began 10 years ago, has progressively worsened. There was no history of trauma. Two years after the onset of pain, she noticed a swelling on the lateral side of her left knee joint, which gradually increased in size and was associated with a reduction in knee joint movement. Activities that involved bending the knee triggered the pain. The patient has no significant past medical or family history. Physical examination revealed swelling on the lateral aspect of the left knee, without any localized redness or increased temperature. The swelling, measuring 15×5 cm, extended from the lateral aspect of the left distal femur to the fibular head. It was bony, hard, not attached to the underlying bone, and non-tender. The range of motion in the left knee joint was limited to 0–40°, with further movement restricted. There was no evidence of neurovascular impairment in the distal part of the limb. No similar lesions were found elsewhere on the body.

A 2-year-old female child, third in birth order, born to parents who are first cousins, delivered vaginally at term without complications, presented with sparse scalp hair, absent body hair, eyebrows, and eyelashes, and delayed tooth eruption. The mother reported that the child does not sweat and has had recurrent fevers since the neonatal period. Psychomotor development was normal. There was no family history of similar symptoms, except for a previous female sibling with similar complaints and a distinctive appearance, who experienced recurrent fever and chest infections during the neonatal period and died at 6 months of age. The parents' first pregnancy ended in a miscarriage at 2 months for unknown reasons. Skin examination showed smooth, normal-looking skin with normal palmoplantar skin and dermatoglyphics. The child had an aged appearance with finely wrinkled skin around the eyes, an everted lower lip, and low-set ears. Scalp hair was sparse, light-colored, and thin, with complete absence of eyelashes, eyebrows, and body hair. Oral examination revealed only two conical, widely spaced maxillary teeth and a low-arched, flat palate. Nails were normal. Systemic examination showed no abnormalities, and there was no evidence of mental retardation. Imaging of the jaw showed a lack of teeth with only two maxillary teeth and a left mandibular tooth bud. Biochemical tests were normal.

A 5-month-old girl presented with inadequate weight gain, poor feeding, vomiting, and diarrhea. She was born to first-degree cousins and had a history of sibling death. Laboratory tests revealed pancytopenia, necessitating transfusions, along with lymphopenia and hypogammaglobulinemia. Lymphocyte subset percentages were within normal limits. Serum vitamin B12 level was low (136 pg/mL), and serum homocysteine level was not measurable. CMV PCR test was positive. The patient received intravenous immunoglobulin, ganciclovir, and prophylactic antibacterial and antifungal treatments. Bone marrow aspiration indicated significant vacuolization in the myeloid lineage and notable megaloblastic changes. Molecular analysis did not confirm Pearson syndrome. The patient was administered intramuscular vitamin B12 and oral folic acid, resulting in clinical and hematological improvement. Following the discontinuation of vitamin B12 therapy by her family, she experienced a relapse with severe pancytopenia, leading to the resumption of vitamin B12 treatment.

orpha_name: Glycogen storage disease due to acid maltase deficiency, infantile onset
text: A 7-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital following an abnormal screening result. She was born at full-term with a birth weight of 4380 grams. The parents reported no issues with feeding, activity levels, or crying strength. Newborn screening was conducted on her third day of life. On Day 4, the laboratory received her sample. By Day 6, results indicated a high-risk condition with GAA activity measured at 0.18 uM/h (critical cutoff < 0.5) and a ratio of 42.87 (critical cutoff acid β-glucosidase (ABG)/GAA ≥ 20). An urgent visit was arranged for Day 7. Upon examination, the newborn exhibited normal muscle power, normal reflexes, no macroglossia, but a slight decrease in facial folds.

During a routine prenatal ultrasound screening in the 11th gestational week (GW), a 39-year-old woman was found to have a fetus with a giant omphalocele containing almost the entire liver. MRI in the 30th GW confirmed a covered abdominal wall defect without associated malformations. In the 37th GW, a caesarean section was performed. At birth, the male neonate presented with a covered abdominal wall defect of 7 cm, mostly occupied by the liver. An abdominal and cardiac ultrasound ruled out major associated malformations. The boy was scheduled for a staged closure. During surgery, a mass in the left liver was noted and mistaken for a hematoma. A fascial Gore-Tex patch measuring 5 × 5 cm was applied. On the 31st postnatal day, the patient’s general condition progressively worsened. Echocardiographic examinations showed worsening of the cardiac biventricular function due to herniation of the right liver into the chest. On Day 38 of life, closure of the anterior diaphragmatic hernia through the abdominal wound was performed. After reduction of the liver and resection of the CDH sac, absence of the pericardium was noted. The diaphragmatic defect of 5 × 4 cm was closed with a Gore-Tex patch and the abdominal wall defect was covered with a Gore-Tex patch. In the following 3 weeks, staged closure was continued without much progress, mostly because of the lesion in the left liver. Work-up of this lesion by ultrasound and MRI demonstrated a 5.5 × 5.1 × 5.5 cm tumor in segment III of the liver consistent with the diagnosis of a vascular tumor. The attempted therapy with propranolol (1 mg/kg/day) was ineffective and was discontinued due to the onset of bradycardia. Finally, surgical resection of the liver tumor was decided. At surgery, the well-defined mass was completely resected. While the abdominal fascia was closed using a Gore-Tex patch of 3 × 4 cm, the skin was closed directly.

A 40-year-old man was referred from the Department of Thoracic Surgery with visual loss noticed in the recovery room after undergoing video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS). The procedure involved a posterior segmentectomy of the right upper lobe and left lower lobe for lung masses under general anesthesia. The surgery was uneventful, lasting approximately 2 hours, with no significant fluctuations in blood pressure or notable blood loss. Histologic examination revealed malignant lymphoma and reactive hyperplasia in the surrounding lymph nodes. Post-surgery, his blood pressure, hemoglobin, glucose, and cholesterol levels were all within normal ranges. Ophthalmologic evaluation immediately after surgery showed visual acuities of hand motion in both eyes (OU). Pupils were reactive without any relative afferent pupillary defect, and eye motility was full OU. Slit lamp examination of the eyelid, anterior chamber, and fundus examination were normal.

A 52-year-old male patient presented with arthritis, eye pain, itching, and redness. He had a history of allergic rhinitis and was not on any medication prior to evaluation. Laboratory results showed serum urea at 102.1 mg/dL (12.84–42.8 mg/dL), creatinine at 4.66 mg/dL (0.70–1.20 mg/dL), eGFR at 13 mL/min/1.73 m2 (60–120 mL/min/1.73 m2), serum albumin at 30.4 gr/L (35–52 gr/L), blood eosinophil count at 1.23 103/μL (0.03–0.59 103/μL), and C-reactive protein at 69.8 mg/L (0–5 mg/L). Urine analysis showed 1+ proteinuria, with no hematuria or leukocyturia, and normal urine microscopy. A 24-hour urine collection revealed 1527.5 mg/day proteinuria. Additional laboratory findings included C3c at 0.38 g/L (0.9–1.8 g/L), C4 at < 0.02 g/L (0.1–0.4 g/L), serum amyloid A at 50.70 mg/dL (5.7–8.2 mg/dL), IgG at 37.83 g/L (7–16 g/L), α1 globulin at 6% (2.9–4.9), β1 globulin at 3.2% (4.7–7.2), β2 globulin at 7.8% (3.2–6.5), and γ globulin at 39.4% (11.1–18.8). Liver and thyroid function tests, as well as pancreatic enzymes, were normal. Blood and urine cultures showed no growth. Rheumatoid factor and autoantibody tests were negative. Contrast-enhanced abdominal computed tomography revealed an inflamed appearance in the adipose tissue in the left pararectal region, loss of corticomedullary separation, and hypodense patchy areas in both kidneys. The respiratory system examination was normal, there were no rashes, and antibodies (ANA, pANCA, cANCA, antiGBM, etc.) were negative. There was no clinical picture of rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

A 19-year-old male presented to Chigateri General Hospital in April 2020 with joint pain for one year and generalized weakness for 20 days. He reported progressive, painless blurring of vision over the past two months, more pronounced in the right eye. There was no history of photophobia, lachrymation, or trauma. He has been managing diabetes mellitus for six years with insulin, metformin, and pioglitazone. His parents had a third-degree consanguineous marriage, and his sibling is unaffected. A maternal cousin has similar skin lesions. His mother did not receive antenatal care and had a domicile delivery. Immunization history is inconsistent. There was no history of palpitation, lower limb swelling, syncope, reduced urine output, hypertension, congenital heart disease, kidney disease, hypothyroidism, or other illnesses. The patient appeared poorly built and undernourished. Vital signs were normal, with no pallor, icterus, cyanosis, clubbing, lymphadenopathy, or edema. His body mass index was 15.4 kg/m2, middle upper arm circumference was 16.5 cm, and head circumference was 51.5 cm. He had large prominent eyes, coarse facies, malocclusion of the teeth with hyperdontia, dermatitis neglecta with acanthosis nigricans on all sides of the neck, a fissured tongue, and enlarged tonsils. Subungual hyperkeratosis was noted in his fingernails, and acanthosis nigricans was present on the dorsum of the hand extending to the forearm. His toenails showed onychodystrophy and pincer nail. Clinically, his liver was enlarged, and the examination of all other systems was unremarkable.

A 12-year-old male patient presented with a subcutaneous nodule on the anterolateral surface of his right leg. The nodule, first noticed by the patient's family about a month prior, had been growing slowly. On physical examination, the nodule measured approximately 2 cm in diameter, had a well-defined border, and was located within the skin and subcutaneous tissue. It exhibited a bluish discoloration but was not associated with pain or ulceration. An excisional procedure was performed under local anesthesia within a week of the initial presentation. The lesion was removed with 1 to 2 cm margins, including skin and subcutaneous tissue above the fascial plane. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient's legal guardians.

A 7-month-old male infant from Agra, Uttar Pradesh, presented with delayed developmental milestones since birth and an increase in head size over the past 2 months following a febrile illness. The child was born vaginally at term with a birth weight of 1.25 kg, and the birth and perinatal period were uneventful. He had not achieved neck holding, social smile, or cooing. At 2 months old, he experienced a febrile illness requiring a 25-day hospital stay, during which he had multiple seizures characterized by whole-body tightening and a vacant stare. He was treated with multiple antibiotics and antiepileptic drugs and was discharged on antiepileptic medications, with no further seizures reported since discharge. Persistent flaky scalp lesions were noted despite treatment. The mother observed a gradual increase in the child's head size since the febrile episode, along with excessive irritability. There were apparent visual and auditory impairments. The child was the fourth in birth order from a non-consanguineous marriage. The first child had died at 1.5 years of age from a similar illness, the second child is a healthy 5-year-old, and the third pregnancy ended in a spontaneous abortion at 3 months of amenorrhea. On examination, the child appeared small for age with a large head, weighing 4.6 kg and measuring 62 cm in length, with a weight-for-length less than –3 standard deviation (SD). Head circumference was 48 cm, greater than +3 SD. He had short, sparse, coarse, lightly pigmented hair with seborrheic dermatitis on the scalp and a depressed nasal bridge. Neurological examination revealed irritability, lack of interaction with surroundings or parents, absence of visual fixation, and no response to auditory stimuli. Muscle tone was low in all four limbs, with symmetrical movement. Deep tendon reflexes were brisk, and plantar reflexes were upgoing. Fundus examination was normal, with no nystagmus. There was no hepatosplenomegaly, and the rest of the systemic examination was unremarkable. Hematological parameters were normal except for mild anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL. Serum lactate levels were elevated. MRI of the brain performed during the febrile illness showed hyperintense signals in the subcortical and deep white matter of the bilateral temporal regions, with changes suggestive of megalencephalic leukodystrophy. Reexamination of the MRI films revealed tortuous blood vessels.

A 33-year-old male with no significant past medical history presented with a two-day history of dystonic posturing affecting his left hand and arm. The episodes involved bending of the elbow and specific finger posturing, without any oculo-bulbar or sensory symptoms. These episodes occurred randomly throughout the day and were later found to be triggered by caffeine consumption. The duration of the episodes varied from several minutes to one instance lasting nine hours. The episodes were painful, with the patient unable to voluntarily relax the affected arm and hand. Mental clarity was maintained throughout and after the episodes, which ceased during sleep. Initially, the symptoms were confined to the left arm but later spread to the right arm, occurring independently in both arms at random intervals. The patient reported no family history of movement disorders and did not smoke, consume alcohol, or take any medications. On examination, his blood pressure was 129/79 mmHg, heart rate was 77 beats per minute, weight was 195 pounds (88.5 kilograms), height was six feet (182.9 centimeters), and body-mass index was 26.4. His speech was normal, and his gait was unremarkable. Cranial nerve examination was normal, with intact ocular motion and no nystagmus. Facial strength was symmetric and full, with no oro-buccolingual dyskinesias. Cervical musculature examination showed no cervical dystonia, and oropharyngeal examination revealed a brisk gag reflex without palatal myoclonus. Muscle strength in both upper and lower extremities was graded 5/5, and deep tendon reflexes were 2+ and symmetric. There was no spasticity, and Hoffman and finger flexor reflexes were absent bilaterally. No cogwheeling was detected at the wrist. The Babinski sign was absent with bilateral plantar strokes. Finger sequencing, finger-to-nose, and heel-to-shin maneuvers were accurate and symmetric bilaterally. During the clinic visit, an episode was observed, starting with a 90-degree flexion of the left elbow and extending to the hand. The posture involved flexion of the first, third, and fourth fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints with extension of the proximal and distal interphalangeal joints, while the second and fifth fingers remained extended, resembling a bovine longhorn.

A male aged 9 years and 9 months presented to the Emergency Department with a one-week history of increased urinary frequency and thirst. He reported decreased appetite and had an unintentional weight loss of 7 lbs over the past week. Earlier that day, his primary care physician noted an elevated blood glucose finger stick at 138 mg/dL and urinalysis showed glucosuria with 100 mg/dL. He was referred to the Emergency Department for further care. His birth history included prematurity with induction at 36 weeks due to intrauterine growth restriction, and delivery via Cesarean section due to fetal distress. His birth weight was 2690 grams and birth length was 48.3 cm. He was monitored in the neonatal intensive care unit for 24 hours for potential hypoglycemia due to maternal gestational diabetes, but his blood glucose levels remained normal. He had jaundice but did not require phototherapy and passed his newborn hearing screen before discharge. His past medical history included an endocrine evaluation at 4 years and 3 months of age for polyuria, polydipsia, and weight gain. His weight was stable in the 50th percentile until approximately 2 years of age when it increased to the 90th percentile, reaching the 99th percentile at the time of his endocrinology visit. His height decreased from the 25th percentile at 3 years of age to approximately the 15th percentile at the time of his endocrinology visit. Evaluation revealed an elevated fasting blood glucose level of 113 mg/dL and mildly elevated thyroid stimulating hormone at 5.3 mIU/L (normal range 0.5–4.3 mIU/L). His serum creatinine was 0.41 mg/dL and electrolytes were unremarkable. Further studies for thyroid function and Cushing's syndrome were recommended but not completed. He had a history of multiple sinus and ear infections requiring adenoidectomy and four sets of myringotomy tubes. He experienced speech delay due to frequent ear infections and received speech therapy twice weekly and hearing therapy three times weekly. He had physical and occupational therapy services until kindergarten for delayed milestones, including walking at 16 months of age. He receives additional support for reading, and formal academic testing was recommended by his school but not yet completed. An immunologic evaluation at an outside hospital due to frequent infections was unremarkable. He was diagnosed with asthma at 1 year of age and was prescribed fluticasone and cetirizine. His family history included maternal renal injury secondary to diabetes during pregnancy, photophobia in his mother, nephrolithiasis in the maternal grandfather and maternal aunt, hypertension in the maternal grandfather, and hearing loss and photophobia in a first cousin on the maternal side. There was no family history of consanguinity. Review of systems revealed subjective chills and constipation. He also reported waking at night to drink and void but denied fever, abdominal pain, daytime or nighttime enuresis, urgency, dysuria, inability to empty his bladder, or hematuria. In the Emergency Department, his blood pressure was 123/71 mmHg, weight was 37 kg (82nd percentile), and height was 127.5 cm (3rd percentile). Physical examination revealed a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur and hypogonadism. There were concerns for photophobia during his eye exam. No other dysmorphic features were noted. Initial laboratory work-up showed elevated serum creatinine of 4.4 mg/dL, low serum calcium of 7.7 mg/dL (8.8–10.1 mg/dL), and slightly elevated serum phosphorus of 6.1 mg/dL (3.7–5.6 mg/dL). Serum intact parathyroid hormone level was elevated at 340 pg/mL (9–52 pg/mL). Complete blood count revealed normocytic anemia with serum hemoglobin of 8.1 g/dL (11.5–15.5 g/dL). Urinalysis showed moderate blood, no proteinuria, no leukocyte esterase, and a specific gravity of ≤1.005. Renal/bladder ultrasound showed small kidneys with right kidney 6.6 cm and left kidney 7.8 cm with increased echogenicity bilaterally and no evidence of cystic disease. An echocardiogram was performed due to the murmur, which was unremarkable. Ophthalmology exam showed no cystine crystals but revealed bilateral retinal dystrophy with 20/50 OD and 20/30 OS. Bone age assessment indicated it was greater than 2 standard deviations above his chronological age.

The patient is a 35-year-old pregnant woman, gravida 4, para 1, with a history of two abortions in the first and second trimesters. Her first pregnancy ended in a spontaneous first-trimester miscarriage. In 2013, her second pregnancy was complicated by oligohydramnios, and she underwent a planned cesarean section in the third trimester, delivering a healthy male baby three weeks before the expected date. In 2015, her third pregnancy was terminated at 16 weeks, followed by surgical evacuation after a failed induction. Further details about this pregnancy are unavailable. The patient presented to the emergency department with shortness of breath that had progressively worsened over a few days and was exacerbated by moderate exertion. She was 16 weeks pregnant at the time, despite previous advice against pregnancy. She reported no chest pain, productive cough, fever, hemoptysis, dizziness, abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, or other cardiorespiratory or genitourinary symptoms. Her medications included aspirin 100 mg once daily and folic acid 5 mg daily. On examination, she was afebrile with a blood pressure of 112/74 mmHg and a pulse rate of 101 bpm. Her chest was clear with normal first and second heart sounds, and a pansystolic murmur radiating to the carotids was noted. Laboratory investigations revealed normal cardiac markers, creatinine, electrolyte and lipid profiles, and normal liver and thyroid function tests. Her complete blood count showed mild leukocytosis (white blood cell count of 13.30 × 10^3/uL), iron-deficiency anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL, and a normal platelet count. NT-proBNP was mildly elevated at 547.2 pg/mL. An electrocardiogram indicated sinus tachycardia and left ventricular hypertrophy with a strain pattern. A chest X-ray was normal.

disease: Idiopathic Infantile Hypercalcemia due to CYP24A1 mutation
text: A 21-month-old girl was admitted to the hospital due to excessive thirst and poor growth. For the past five months, she had been drinking large amounts of fluids both day and night and showed a decreased interest in eating solid foods. Attempts to limit her fluid intake resulted in her refusal to eat. Blood tests ruled out diabetes mellitus, and urine concentration tests ruled out diabetes insipidus. Her medical history included a healthy pregnancy and birth, but she experienced severe vomiting during the first seven months of life. She was breastfed with supplemental formula feedings due to persistent hunger. After starting solid foods, her hunger and vomiting decreased. There was no family history of similar issues, and her parents were of Iranian and Danish descent with no consanguinity. She was not on any medication except for the recommended 400 IU/day of vitamin D. Physical examination was normal, with no signs of syndromic features or physical disease. Her growth chart showed normal height for her age, but her weight had dropped by one standard deviation over the past three months. Blood tests upon hospitalization revealed elevated levels of total calcium (3.42 mmol/L; Ref. range 2.17-2.66 mmol/L) and free calcium (1.68 mmol/L; Ref. range 1.18-1.32 mmol/L). Parathyroid hormone (PTH) was undetectable (<4 ng/L; Ref. range 14.0-72.0 ng/L). Levels of 25 vitamin-D and 1.25 vitamin-D were in the high-to-normal range, with 25 vitamin-D at 107 nmol/L (Ref. range >50 nmol/L) and 1.25 vitamin-D at 146 pmol/L (Ref. range 51-177 pmol/L). Serum phosphate, liver, kidney, and thyroid tests were normal. Kidney ultrasound showed medullary nephrocalcinosis. Urine tests indicated hypercalciuria with a Ca/creatinine ratio of 1.9 mmol/mmol (Ref. range: <0.7 mmol/mmol). There were no indications of other diseases or malignancies. Blood calcium levels were tested in both parents, with the mother showing a calcium level of 2.53 mmol/L (Ref. range: 2.15-2.51 mmol/L), which was considered normal. No further examinations, including renal ultrasound or renal testing, were conducted.

disease: Multiple Primary Melanoma associated with CDKN2A Mutation
orpha_name: Familial atypical multiple mole melanoma syndrome
text: A 72-year-old Caucasian female from an urban area with over 200,000 residents and an average annual sunshine of 2299 hours (latitude 44°10′50.63′′ N and longitude 28°38′3.55′′ E) visited the Dermatology Clinic of an academic hospital in August 2022. She reported recent changes in multiple melanocytic lesions on various skin areas. Her family history included essential arterial hypertension and diabetes mellitus in her mother and prostate cancer in her father, with no significant skin conditions in close relatives. The patient had personal medical conditions such as arterial hypertension and dyslipidemia. She was retired, having previously worked as a call center representative with no professional ultraviolet radiation exposure. Her sun-damage risk factors included Fitzpatrick phototype II and a history of childhood sunburns. There were no issues related to healthcare access, cultural, or financial matters. Clinical and dermoscopic assessments revealed Fitzpatrick phototype II and approximately 30 pigmented nevi with irregular shapes, varying colors (light to dark brown), and different sizes. Total body mapping (TBM) was used for photographic documentation of the entire body surface, followed by sequential digital dermoscopy (FotoFinder bodystudio ATBM master system, 4th Generation, FotoFinder Systems GmbH, Bad Birnbach, Germany) of specific melanocytic lesions to monitor their changes over time and identify any new lesions. Four melanocytic lesions showed features such as irregular borders, shiny white streaks, and an irregular pigmented network, leading to the decision for surgical excision.

A 12-year-old male was referred to our center in Damascus. His parents are not related by blood. The patient has a history of pallor and anemia. Laboratory results showed hemoglobin levels with Hb A1 at 36.2%, Hb F at 62.2%, and Hb A2 at 1.6%. His father exhibited characteristics consistent with a specific hemoglobin trait, while his mother had normal indices but with Hb A2 levels at 1.9%. The patient occasionally requires blood transfusions, whereas his parents have never needed transfusions. Hematological parameters for the patient and his parents were obtained using an automated differential cell counter. Hemoglobin analysis was conducted using a capillary electrophoresis system.

disease: Steven Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis  overlap syndrome due to methazolamide
text: An 85-year-old Chinese male was admitted to the infectious diseases department with pruritic rashes and fever on October 17, 2019. His medical history includes cerebral infarction, left-eye glaucoma, and right-eye blindness. He reported no drug or food allergies. His daughter had a history of labial ulceration after taking sulfanilamide tablets, which resolved within 2 days after stopping the medication. Twelve days prior to admission, he was prescribed methazolamide (25 mg tablets, twice daily for 10 days) for glaucoma. One day after starting the medication, he developed swollen red eyes and was diagnosed with corneal injury and keratitis at a local hospital. He was treated with levofloxacin eye drops and additional medications (carteolol eye drops, furan thiamine tablets, and pilocarpine eye drops) for glaucoma. Six days before admission, he developed a fever with a peak temperature of 39.9°C. Three days later, he noticed itching pale red macules with blister formations starting from the abdomen, genitals, hands, and soles, which quickly spread over his body. He revisited the local hospital, where a dermatologist noted severe erythema multiforme. Blood tests showed a white blood cell count of 6.3 × 10^9/L (normal range 3.5–9.5 × 10^9/L), neutrophil ratio of 84.6% (normal range 40–70%), and procalcitonin of 0.1 ng/mL (normal range <0.1 ng/mL). Chest computed tomography revealed bilateral pneumonia. He was treated with cefuroxime and received supportive and symptomatic care, but his fever and rashes did not improve. Upon admission to our hospital, he was awake and alert with a temperature of 38.7°C, pulse of 80 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, and blood pressure of 107/68 mmHg. His eyes were red, swollen, and filled with fluid. He had glaucoma in his left eye with a pupil diameter of 3 mm, sensitivity to light reflexes, and a visual acuity of 0.2. His right eye was blind. He had scattered itching erythematous macules coalescing into papules and blisters all over his body, with no epidermal detachment. Ulcerations were present in the oral mucosa and right foot. Nikolsky's sign was positive. Pulmonary, cardiac, abdominal, and neurological examinations were otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed a white blood cell count of 6.5 × 10^9/L, neutrophil percentage of 79.4%, C-reactive protein of 30.6 mg/L (normal range 0–10 mg/L), and procalcitonin of 0.1 ng/mL.

An 18-year-old Korean male presented to the Department of Dermatology at Yeungnam University Hospital on November 2, 2005, with progressively generalized pigmented lesions. He was reported to be normal at birth. Reticular hyperpigmented lesions were first noted at the age of 2 on the popliteal and antecubital fossae. These changes gradually spread to the axillae and trunk. Linear sclerosing bands appeared on both the antecubital and popliteal fossae over the years. The lesions increased in size and number over time, but the patient remained asymptomatic. His family history revealed that his father had similar symptoms and passed away in his forties due to cardiovascular disease. His younger brother also exhibited similar poikilodermatous lesions, which appeared at the age of 3 and showed progressive hyperpigmented and sclerotic changes. Physical examination showed micrognathia, clubbing of the fingers, and slim extremities compared to the trunk. Dermatologic examination revealed generalized hyperpigmented, telangiectatic, and atrophic changes, particularly pronounced in the axillae, antecubital, and popliteal fossae. Linear hyperkeratotic and sclerotic bands were observed on both the antecubital and popliteal fossae. Sclerosis of the palms and soles was also noted. Laboratory tests, including complete blood cell count, liver function test, blood coagulation test, antinuclear antibody, and urinalysis, were within normal limits or negative.

A 23-month-old boy was admitted to the hospital following a 5-day period of stridor, cough, and hoarseness. Upon admission, he was alert and in good spirits, with an oxygen saturation of 96% on room air, a body temperature of 36.8°C, a respiratory rate of 34 breaths per minute, and a heart rate of 116 beats per minute. Physical examination revealed retractions in the suprasternal fossa, supraclavicular fossa, and intercostal spaces during inhalation. Inspiratory stridor and moist rales were audible in the lungs, while the cardiac examination was normal. The boy was born full-term via vaginal delivery with no complications during birth, no adverse events, and no history of tracheal intubation or ventilator use. His mother had a normal pregnancy, and his growth and development were typical. However, he exhibited stridor, weak crying, and hoarseness from birth. The family doctor initially recommended vitamin D and calcium supplements, which led to some improvement in symptoms, though they never fully resolved. A chest X-ray taken after admission showed bronchopneumonia. Following treatment with glucocorticoids and antibiotics, the boy's breathing improved, his cough subsided, hoarseness decreased, and the moist rales gradually disappeared. However, inspiratory stridor persisted, particularly after physical activity or crying.

A 5-month-old female infant exhibited horizontal and rotational nystagmus of pendular characteristics, which occasionally rebounded and disappeared when focusing on near objects. The nystagmus was more pronounced in the hyperopic left eye. Fundoscopic examination was normal in both eyes at that time. Electroencephalography showed sharp theta waves, nystagmus episodes, and slow delta waves during head movements originating from the parietal and occipital lobes. Brain imaging via computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was normal. Later in life, the patient developed T2DM and obesity. Over a 5-year period (2016 to 2021), her metabolic profile, including vitamin B12, glucose profile (HbA1c, glucose tolerance tests), liver function tests, and complete blood count, were normal except for low levels of vitamin D3 at various times. Abdominal ultrasound showed no abnormalities in the liver, common bile duct, portal vein, gallbladder, pancreas, spleen, or kidneys. Visual acuity was limited to light perception in both eyes and she received different diagnoses from various hospitals. At 17 years old, she presented to our department in 2021. Best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was light perception in both eyes, reportedly since the age of 13. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) scans of the macular region showed significant disruption of the photoreceptor layer in both eyes, with minimal preservation around the central fovea. Fluorescein angiography (FA) revealed vessel attenuation and hyperfluorescent areas in the posterior pole. Autofluorescence (FAF) indicated extensive disruption of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) metabolic activity in the posterior pole and periphery of both eyes. Electroretinogram (ERG) and visual evoked potentials (VEP) were abnormal, with recordings not surpassing electrical noise.

An 11-year-old boy with no significant medical history was admitted due to a 4-month history of headache, general condition decline, apathy, increased urination, constipation, swelling of feet and hands, and reduced muscle strength. Laboratory tests indicated mild anemia, iron deficiency, elevated eosinophil count, increased ESR, low vitamin D levels, elevated gamma globulin levels, high calcium levels, low phosphate levels, and increased urea and creatinine concentrations. A high calcium creatinine index and reduced parathormone levels were noted. MRI of the head showed no abnormalities. Abdominal ultrasound revealed thickening of the renal parenchymal layer and kidney enlargement. Thyroid and parathyroid ultrasound showed no abnormalities. The patient was treated with furosemide and infusion fluids without improvement. A subfebrile state was also noted. Further examinations included an abdominal ultrasound that continued to show kidney enlargement with a hyperechogenic signal of the renal parenchyma and liver enlargement. Whole-body MRI revealed hepatomegaly and a hyperintense signal in the kidneys and lower limb fascia. Bone scintigraphy and nailfold capillaroscopy showed no abnormalities. Bone marrow biopsy ruled out proliferative diseases.

disease: Hereditary Sensory and Autonomic Neuropathy Type II
orpha_name: Hereditary Sensory and Autonomic Neuropathy Type II
text: A 54-year-old woman exhibited a loss of touch, temperature, position, and vibration sense, along with a taste disorder and insensitivity to pain at the body surface since infancy. She also experienced symptoms of an autonomic disorder, including orthostatic hypotension, body temperature fluctuations, and lack of urge to defecate. Despite normal mental growth and development, she did not walk until 18 months of age. Her family history was unremarkable, with a healthy younger sister and non-consanguineous parents. During childhood, she had repeated injuries due to pain insensitivity and was diagnosed with Freiberg disease at 6 years. She experienced frequent fainting episodes and was diagnosed with an autonomic nervous system imbalance at 21 years. She perspired on her back even in cold temperatures and did not require a snowsuit. She did not have high blood pressure. At 14 years, after repeated jumping in gym class, she developed severe blisters on the sole of her left foot, which progressed to metatarsal osteomyelitis. At 18 years, a left metatarsal was replaced by an autogenous bone graft due to refractory osteomyelitis, taking approximately 10 years for full recovery. She had migraines from childhood until 20 years, managed with daily antipyrine medication. She could feel toothache and headache but lacked surface sensation on her face, except around the jaw. Sensation detection showed a “stocking and glove distribution” with a gradient from periphery to center. She could perceive visceral pain, such as menstrual or gastroenteritis pain, managed with antipyrine medication. At 42 years, she began using a cane for walking outside. Despite recognizing her different pain perception since childhood, she and her parents did not consult a doctor about it until she was 48 years old. At 48 years, she developed sudden severe pain and swelling in her right joint, and her previous physician diagnosed Charcot arthropathy. At 50 years, she was confined to a wheelchair to avoid weight-bearing on her joints. Over the subsequent years, pain gradually affected multiple joints. NSAIDs provided limited relief for her joint pain. At 54 years, her joint pain became intolerable, and NSAIDs were insufficient for daily activities. Mexiletine hydrochloride treatment was ineffective, and she experienced side effects from pregabalin and duloxetine. She was subsequently referred to a multidisciplinary center for pain management.

A 26-year-old male visited Tianjin Medical University General Hospital due to a painless neck lump, first noticed 6 years ago, which had recently grown rapidly. The patient experienced agitation, irritability, excessive sweating, and discomfort while swallowing. Previously, he had been diagnosed with nodular goiter by other hospitals and had intermittently taken Euthyrox® for several years. He also reported childhood hearing impairment and language learning difficulties, though clinical examination indicated normal mental and physical development. Physical examination revealed a visible grade III goiter, with an enlarged, tough thyroid without palpable nodules and a clear boundary. Laboratory tests showed a free thyroxine level of 9.99 pmol/l (normal range: 11.5–23.5 pmol/l), a free triiodothyronine level of 6.07 pmol/l (normal range: 3.5–6.5 pmol/l), and a thyroid stimulating hormone level of 2.401 μlU/ml (normal range: 0.3–5.0 μlU/ml). Thyrotrophin receptor, thyroglobulin, thyroperoxidase antibodies, carcinoembryonic antigen, thyroglobulin, and calcitonin levels were within normal ranges. A chest X-ray showed tracheal narrowing at the superior thoracic aperture, and a thyroid ultrasound revealed multiple hypoechoic signals in each lobe and isthmus of the thyroid. Cervical lymph node enlargement was also noted. Cervical computed tomography scan showed diffuse enlargement of each lobe and thickening of the thyroid isthmus, with numerous oval, low- and slightly high-density nodules in the thyroid. Electrical capacitance tomography results were consistent with signs of a nodular goiter.

A 23-year-old right-handed female presented on December 8, 2021, with chronic bilateral foot arch pain, and paroxysmal diffuse spine and neck pain for nine months. The pain began in March 2021 following a streptococcal throat infection confirmed by a positive strep test. Post-recovery, she experienced sharp pain in her right leg extending to her right shin, later involving her left leg. The pain gradually spread to her entire spine and hips, increasing with movement and ambulation. The pain was described as dull (3/10) and shooting (8/10). She also reported intermittent numbness in both feet and tailbone, worsening fatigue, intermittent dizziness triggered by standing and walking, blurry vision in both eyes, numbness and tingling in her legs, and memory difficulties related to pain. She had no muscle weakness, vertigo, headache, seizure, or other constitutional symptoms. Her medical history included anemia, anxiety, and depression, with no significant surgical history. She had allergies to cat hair, dust mites, and penicillin, and was an active smoker with a 1.25-pack year history over five years. There was no family history of epilepsy. She had a head trauma at age 17 from a fall off a horse, resulting in loss of consciousness. No brain infections or generalized tonic-clonic seizures were reported. On examination, she was alert, oriented, and in mild distress, appearing anxious. Deep tendon reflexes were symmetrical but brisk in both arms and legs. Her natural and tandem gait were unsteady, and heel-walking and toe-walking were difficult. There was tenderness over the paravertebral points of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine, with limited range of motion in the spine. Routine blood tests were normal. X-rays of the cervical spine showed vertebral body heights, disc spaces, and facet joints with a mild reversal of the usual cervical lordosis without spondylolisthesis. X-rays of the thoracic and lumbar spine showed minimal levocurvature of the lumbar spine. On January 23, 2022, she experienced a syncope episode after standing for about 20 minutes while smoking. She developed nausea, ringing in her ears, and dimming vision before losing consciousness for less than a minute. Her face reportedly turned gray before collapsing, but she did not hit her head. Post-episode, she had reduced sensation in her left arm for a few days and paresthesia in all extremities that night. She denied convulsions, tongue biting, and incontinence but reported headaches, nausea, drowsiness, and persistent weakness and numbness. She had difficulty understanding speech immediately after the episode and felt fully recovered after one to two hours. This was an isolated event with no recurrence and no cardiac symptoms. She was referred to a cardiologist and neurologist. An electrocardiogram (EKG), ultrasound (US) duplex carotid artery bilateral, and a routine electroencephalogram (EEG) awake and drowsy were performed. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. The EKG suggested possible left atrial enlargement, likely a false positive. The US duplex carotid arteries showed a mean carotid intima-media thickness (CIMT) below the 25th percentile for her age and gender. An echocardiogram (echo) was normal, ruling out structural heart diseases as a cause of syncope.

A 44-year-old African female with no past medical history presented with Raynaud’s phenomenon, fingertip pain and ulcerations, nail fold capillary changes, facial and back rash, skin tightness and depigmentation, joint pain, morning stiffness, and fatigue. Physical examination showed hypopigmented scarring skin lesions on the face and neck and tenderness and swelling of small joints in the hands and wrists. The laboratory results were as follows: antinuclear antibody (ANA) + (1:80, speckled), anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70) antibody + (4.8 U/mL), cardiolipin IgM + (16 MPL U/mL), and smooth muscle antibody (SMA) + (1:20), and anti-Sjögren’s syndrome-related antigen A (anti-Ro/SSA) antibody −, anti-Sjögren’s syndrome type B (anti-La/SSB) antibody −, antinuclear ribonucleoprotein (anti-RNP) antibody −, anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-ds DNA) antibody −, and anti-Smith antibody −. Flow cytometry and bone marrow biopsy were negative. Steroids, mycophenolate mofetil, and hydroxychloroquine were started. After four days of steroids and immunosuppressants, the patient’s fatigue, joint pain, and fingertip pain improved during the treatment, but she developed a severe thunderclap headache with left lower extremity weakness.

A seven-year-old girl was seen at The Department of Neurosurgery, Yuquan Hospital, Tsinghua University (Beijing, China) with a three-year history of episodic convulsions accompanied by loss of consciousness and worsening symptoms. The seizures primarily included absence seizures and generalized tonic-clonic seizures. The main clinical presentation involved a sudden bowing motion (slightly forwards and downwards, to the left) occurring either singly or in clusters. Occasionally, the body would slowly dip to the left and then to the right while the upper and lower limbs became stiff, indicative of absence seizures. These episodes lasted for about half a minute, not exceeding one minute, before subsiding. Oral medications such as oxcarbazepine, clonazepam, topiramate, and valproate provided limited symptom control, with seizure episodes occurring 4–10 times per month. There was no prior history of difficult birth, lack of oxygen, encephalitis, or any other medical conditions. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient’s family.

This case involves a 17-year-old girl from Ardebil, Iran, who has been hospitalized four times. At birth, she appeared normal except for congenital malformations of the great toes. Her parents were not related, and there is no family history of genetic disease. She was born via normal vaginal delivery with a normal birth weight. Throughout her growth, her weight remained at the minimal normal range without high levels of physical activity. At age 11, she was first hospitalized due to the onset of reduced motor function in the shoulders, back, neck, and left knee following trauma. MRI of the right shoulder was normal, but a Tc-99 bone scan showed inflammatory response in the left shoulder and right knee, along with some changes in the left shoulder. Muscle enzyme levels were approximately 1.5 times higher than normal, while other hematologic, hormonal, and biochemical tests were normal. She was treated with low-dose ibuprofen and etidronate (200 mg) every alternate day. At age 16, she was hospitalized again with more severe symptoms, including swelling and stiffness in the right lower limb, and pain in the thigh and right leg. A chest CT scan revealed severe scoliosis, MRI showed a hyper signal lesion in the soft tissue of the lower limb muscles, and an x-ray indicated bone formation in the posterior thigh. Three months later, she was hospitalized with restrictions in the glenohumeral joints on both sides, as well as in the right hip, knee, and ankle, and the left knee. Five months later, at age 17, she was hospitalized for the fourth time due to pain behind her right thigh and was treated with medication and physiotherapy.
